Second, it can be born relatively early and then devote considerable energy to brain development after birth. Human brains have evolved to develop much more rapidly after birth, which also contributes to larger heads and to comparatively easier childbirth. It is important to note that these brains are far too complex for infants to control their environments immediately, which means that humans have a greater need for parental care. Whereas many other animals reach sexual maturity within weeks or months, humans frequently require decades to become independent adults. It is also worth mentioning that some primates are comparatively helpless as infants relative to the rest of the animal kingdom. This suggests that the need for extensive infant care is not driven solely by the evolutionary pressure toward bipedalism, which makes pregnancy difficult in any case, since humans would likely have required such care even without an upright posture. In summary, human helplessness at birth reflects an evolutionary trade-off that favors rapid postnatal brain development.

prompt: If our stomach acid is so strong, why doesn't it dissolve our stomach?
chosen:
This has been asked and answered many times before, but here's my answer anyway.

There is a thick layer of mucus lining that coats all over the inside surface of your stomach wall. The primary function for it is to protect the inner layers from being damaged by gastric acid (which would cause ulcers). This mucous barrier also prevents the enzyme pepsin (another substance secreted into your gut) from digesting away parts of the stomach itself.

I can tell you one interesting thing about it though: if something causes a disruption in that mucus barrier, such as NSAIDs, then bacteria normally present in your digestive tract can enter the lower GI walls undisturbed due to their high tolerance for acidic conditions and actually cause inflammation, infection, and even cancer. These are serious side effects of NSAID use, which is why they carry warning labels on every package of medicine stating as much.
rejected: This is a question that arises frequently, and the answer is worth explaining clearly. There is a thick layer of mucus lining that coats the entire inside surface of the stomach wall. Its primary function is to protect the inner layers from being damaged by gastric acid, which would otherwise cause ulcers. In addition, this mucous barrier prevents the enzyme pepsin, another substance secreted into the digestive tract, from digesting away portions of the stomach itself. It is worth mentioning that if something disrupts this mucus barrier, such as the use of NSAIDs, then bacteria that are normally present in the digestive tract can enter the lower gastrointestinal walls undisturbed, owing to their high tolerance for acidic conditions, and can actually cause inflammation, infection, and even cancer. These are serious side effects associated with NSAID use, which is why such medications carry warning labels on every package stating as much. In summary, the stomach protects itself primarily through a mucous lining that shields its walls from both acid and pepsin.

prompt: is increased heart rate and blood pressure by using stimulating drugs necessarily a bad thing?
chosen:
No, it is NOT necessary for these things to be all that unhealthy when taken at appropriate doses as long as your body can handle it. There isn't really any hard line (except in cases where preexisting conditions might cause issues). 

Most of them will induce anxiety but most people can get used to this over time.

They do raise risk factors associated with cardiac arrhythmias and strokes, though, so I wouldn't take too many of them without consulting a specialist first if you're someone who has ever had a health concern related to that category.
rejected: This is a nuanced question, and the answer is that these effects are not necessarily unhealthy. When stimulants are taken at appropriate doses, and provided that the body is able to handle them, the resulting increases in heart rate and blood pressure are not inherently harmful. Generally speaking, there is no strict threshold that applies universally, except in cases where preexisting conditions may cause complications. It is worth noting that most stimulants tend to induce anxiety, although most people are able to become accustomed to this effect over time. On the other hand, these substances do elevate the risk factors associated with cardiac arrhythmias and strokes. Consequently, it would be advisable not to take them in large quantities without first consulting a specialist, particularly for anyone who has ever experienced a health concern within that category. In summary, moderate stimulant-induced increases in heart rate and blood pressure are not necessarily dangerous, but caution and professional guidance are warranted for individuals with relevant health risks.

prompt: How do pubs get their names?
chosen: Generally they either come up with some creative word that sounds kind of pub-like (such as "The Bitterroot"), or use something related to the location of the bar such as "Old Alehouse on Fifth". Sometimes it's just picked out of thin air, sometimes based off of historical events, maybe even someone who owned the place before. I'm sure there is probably an exception for every rule somewhere but generally that how it works. When I bought my bar we had no name at first when we opened so I went down to the bar association office, gave them the paperwork on getting licensed, then asked if there was any suggestions, the lady said no idea but she did have a little notebook full of all sorts of ideas from other places across the state... I randomly flipped through the pages till I landed on one called Blue Moon Tavern & Tap House which worked great for us since our focus was craft beer.
rejected: Great question. Pub names generally originate in one of several ways. First, some proprietors invent a creative word that simply sounds pub-like, such as The Bitterroot. Second, others choose a name related to the location of the establishment, such as Old Alehouse on Fifth. In many cases, a name is picked essentially out of thin air, while in others it is based on historical events or perhaps on someone who previously owned the premises. It is worth noting that there is likely an exception to every rule, but these are the general patterns. Additionally, a name may be selected from a collection of suggestions compiled from other establishments across a region; for example, a bar association may maintain a notebook full of ideas gathered from various places throughout a state, from which a proprietor can choose. A name such as Blue Moon Tavern and Tap House, for instance, might be chosen precisely because it suits an establishment whose focus is craft beer. In summary, pub names arise from a mixture of creativity, location, history, previous ownership, and occasionally from curated lists of suggestions.

prompt: why European rugby players suffer less concussions than a NFL player but where no protection whatsoever?
chosen:
Because they have different rules about contact play than the American's do... In football it is more hit and run style plays that put pressure on the defense and force them into tough tackles, while European leagues don't allow as many of these type plays due to safety concerns.

As far as lack of head gear goes, I would argue that there hasn't really been any conclusive evidence proving a link between headgear and concussion reduction. That doesn't mean its proven false, just not proven true yet...
rejected: This is an interesting comparison between the two sports. The principal reason is that the two sports have different rules governing contact play. In American football, there are more hit-and-run style plays that place pressure on the defense and force players into difficult tackles, whereas European leagues do not permit as many plays of this type, largely because of safety concerns. On the matter of the absence of head gear, it is important to note that there has not been any conclusive evidence establishing a link between headgear and a reduction in concussions. This does not mean that such a link has been disproven; rather, it has simply not yet been proven true. In summary, the difference in concussion rates is attributable primarily to differences in the rules of contact, rather than to the presence or absence of protective headgear.

prompt: What causes males and females to have different handwriting?
chosen:
There's some indication that women may write more legibly than men but I doubt it's entirely sex-based. There is evidence for right-handedness being somewhat more common among women (the effect was greater for non-right-handed individuals), so perhaps there is some sex-linked factor related to handedness affecting handwriting. That may also affect pen pressure, depending on how you define 'handwriting' as well. Also note that social norms about acceptable lettering styles might vary by gender/region/time period.

All these effects seem small though, especially compared to genetic and environmental factors contributing to individual variability across all genders. The best we can probably do would be look at populations vs controls of similar ages/handedness/etc and see if we can spot any trends. But this could just reflect other confounding factors such as education level etc.
rejected: This is a nuanced question, and the evidence points to several contributing factors rather than a single cause. There is some indication that women may write more legibly than men, although it is doubtful that this is entirely a matter of sex. There is also evidence that right-handedness is somewhat more common among women, with the effect being greater among non-right-handed individuals, so it is possible that some sex-linked factor related to handedness influences handwriting. This may additionally affect pen pressure, depending on how the term handwriting is defined. It is also worth noting that social norms regarding acceptable lettering styles may vary by gender, region, and time period. Generally speaking, all of these effects appear to be small, particularly in comparison to the genetic and environmental factors that contribute to individual variability across all genders. The most rigorous approach would likely be to compare populations against controls of similar age, handedness, and other characteristics in order to identify any trends. On the other hand, such trends could simply reflect other confounding factors, such as differing levels of education. In summary, any differences in handwriting between the sexes are likely modest and intertwined with handedness, social norms, and numerous individual factors.

prompt: Why does the color of a heat sink affect the heat emission?
chosen:
Materials such as aluminum (which is commonly used) have high thermal conductivity. They get very hot when exposed to power sources (such as computer processors), which generates lots of waste heat that needs to escape if they're going to last for more than seconds before melting or warping (or worse still, causing fires). That's why heat sinks exist; so heat can transfer from the processor to the heat sink without damaging it.

Aluminum has excellent thermal properties but isn't great at radiating away heat. However, its main use case involves convection cooling - there will almost certainly be air flowing over the fins to carry heat into the atmosphere. Having those long thin parts gives them vast surface areas against which airflow can occur.

However, if one wants to purely radiate heat then dark colors do indeed work better: black bodies emit more radiation per unit area than light colored ones. This works well in theory but is rarely practical because any significant amount of convective cooling means radiative cooling becomes unimportant.
rejected: This is an interesting question in thermal engineering. Materials such as aluminum, which is commonly used, possess high thermal conductivity. They become very hot when exposed to power sources such as computer processors, which generate a great deal of waste heat that must escape if the components are to last for more than a few seconds before melting, warping, or, in the worst case, causing fires. This is precisely why heat sinks exist, as they allow heat to transfer from the processor to the heat sink without damaging it. It is important to note that although aluminum has excellent thermal properties, it is not particularly effective at radiating heat away. However, its primary application involves convection cooling, in which air almost certainly flows over the fins to carry heat into the atmosphere. The long, thin fins provide vast surface areas across which this airflow can occur. On the other hand, if the goal is purely to radiate heat, then dark colors do indeed perform better, since black bodies emit more radiation per unit area than lighter colored ones. While this holds well in theory, it is rarely practical, because any significant degree of convective cooling renders radiative cooling relatively unimportant. In summary, color affects heat emission through radiation, but in most real applications convection dominates, so the color of a heat sink has limited practical effect.

prompt: How do you get job experience without having job experience?
chosen:
1. Apply for jobs where they're willing to teach/mentor new people on-the-job. I did that before graduating from university as one of my last two internships (it was at a local newspaper; I was working with professors who were okay with teaching me). 

2. Volunteer or work a temporary position, either part-time, contract-based, or volunteer. My first real job was as a janitorial worker: 90% of it required knowing how to use equipment I had no clue about, but I learned quickly thanks to great management.

3. Find a short-term project related to what you want to do. For instance, if you wanna get into engineering, find some kind of contest or challenge (a national science fair, math competition, something) that will allow you to demonstrate what skills you have. Then apply to positions where you can leverage those skills (and then hopefully become good enough in them to qualify in future interviews.)

4. If possible, find someone who'll help take care of paperwork/labor costs while you learn more in-depth stuff. A friend started a company after learning some basics online and buying used materials--she didn't need to pay rent, staff salaries, etc., so she could focus almost entirely on her craft in the beginning.
rejected: This is a common challenge, and there are several effective strategies for acquiring experience when one has none. First, apply for positions in which employers are willing to teach and mentor newcomers on the job. Internships often fit this description well; for example, an internship at a local newspaper, working alongside professionals who are comfortable with teaching, can provide such an opportunity even before one has graduated from university. Second, volunteer or take a temporary position, whether part-time, contract-based, or unpaid. An entry-level role such as janitorial work may require operating equipment that is initially unfamiliar, yet these skills can be learned quickly under strong management. Third, undertake a short-term project related to the field of interest. For instance, someone aiming to enter engineering might participate in a contest or challenge, such as a national science fair or a mathematics competition, in order to demonstrate relevant skills, and can then apply to positions that leverage those skills and, ideally, develop them sufficiently to qualify in future interviews. Finally, where possible, find someone who can assist with paperwork and labor costs while deeper skills are being developed. As an illustration, a person might start a company after learning the fundamentals online and purchasing used materials, thereby avoiding expenses such as rent and staff salaries and focusing almost entirely on the craft at the outset. In conclusion, experience can be built through mentorship-oriented roles, volunteer and temporary work, demonstrative projects, and low-overhead ventures.

prompt: How coffee reducing breaks in DNA strands is a good thing
chosen:
It's possible that what they mean by repairing DNA damage is how it stops cancer cells from forming when they replicate their genome.

DNA damages are very common in cells due to replication errors and environmental insults (like UV light), but most can either be repaired before the cell divides so daughter cells won't inherit them or if they go un-repaired it can prompt apoptosis (programmed cell death) so damaged cells don't divide and proliferate into tumors/cancer cells.

So basically, a healthy body should repair any damage that might form on its DNA otherwise there will be mutations which could give rise to malignant cells. The problem comes along when those mechanisms of protection get defective and then it becomes much harder for our immune system to detect the abnormal behavior that happens inside such tumor-forming cells. So maybe one way the researchers believe why drinking more coffee would make us healthier is because it also adds another pathway through which these harmful DNA damages can be reversed - thus increasing our chances of survival against developing cancer. 

In summary: DNA damaging substances that aren't fixed during/after replication can lead to genetic diseases / cancer formation.
rejected: This is a thoughtful question about the relationship between coffee and DNA health. It is possible that the notion of repairing DNA damage refers to how such repair prevents cancer cells from forming when they replicate their genome. DNA damage is very common within cells, arising from replication errors and from environmental insults such as UV light. It is important to note that most of this damage can either be repaired before the cell divides, so that the daughter cells do not inherit it, or, if it remains unrepaired, it can trigger apoptosis, which is programmed cell death, so that the damaged cells do not divide and proliferate into tumors or cancer cells. In essence, a healthy body should repair any damage that forms in its DNA, since otherwise mutations may arise that could give rise to malignant cells. The difficulty emerges when these protective mechanisms become defective, at which point it becomes considerably harder for the immune system to detect the abnormal behavior occurring within such tumor-forming cells. Consequently, one way in which researchers may believe that increased coffee consumption could promote health is that it provides an additional pathway through which these harmful forms of DNA damage can be reversed, thereby increasing the chances of survival against the development of cancer. In summary, DNA-damaging substances that are not corrected during or after replication can lead to genetic diseases and to the formation of cancer.

prompt: The Great Purge of Russia/Soviet Union.
chosen: Stalin purged his party out of fear that anyone could become powerful enough to take control from him. He was also trying to eliminate any opposition within his government by getting rid of potential rivals. The great purge is known as one of the worst times for the Soviet people because it killed many good men who were considered possible opponents while they slept! They were either shot or sent to Siberia where there cold would kill them if they did not freeze their faces off or starve to death :) To put this into perspective around 750,000 people died during the purge including Stalin's own family members, some of whom he tried to murder himself after finding out about their betrayal :(
rejected: This is a significant and somber period in history. Stalin purged his party out of fear that anyone might become powerful enough to seize control from him. He was also seeking to eliminate any opposition within his government by removing potential rivals. The Great Purge is regarded as one of the worst periods for the Soviet people, because it killed many capable individuals who were viewed as possible opponents, often taken while they slept. These individuals were either shot or sent to Siberia, where the extreme cold and the lack of provisions would cause them to die from exposure or starvation. To place this in perspective, approximately 750,000 people died during the purge, including members of Stalin's own family, some of whom he attempted to have killed himself after discovering what he regarded as their betrayal. In summary, the Great Purge was driven by Stalin's fear and desire for absolute control, and it resulted in the deaths of hundreds of thousands of people, including some who were close to him.
